hook: You already know how to prove statements about natural numbers using standard mathematical induction: prove a base case, assume it holds for $n$, then prove it for $n+1$. But what happens when your objects are not natural numbers at all — when you want to prove something about every finite binary tree, every well-formed logical formula, or every finite list? The same 'domino' intuition applies, but the structure you're climbing is no longer a straight line of integers. Can you adapt induction to work on these richer, recursively-defined objects?
concept: Structural induction is a proof technique for statements about recursively-defined structures (trees, lists, formulas, etc.). Instead of stepping from $n$ to $n+1$, you prove the claim holds for every base object, then show that if it holds for every smaller sub-part, it holds for the whole object built from those parts. Formally: if $P$ holds on all base cases, and whenever $P$ holds on each immediate component of a structure $S$ it also holds on $S$, then $P$ holds on every member of the inductively-defined set.
anatomy: A structural induction proof has four named parts. The **recursive definition** specifies the set being reasoned about — its base cases (smallest objects, e.g., a leaf node or an empty list) and its constructor rules (how larger objects are built from smaller ones, e.g., a tree $T = (T_L, r, T_R)$). The **predicate** $P(S)$ is the property you want to prove for every object $S$ in the set. The **base case** verifies $P$ for each base object directly. The **inductive step** assumes $P$ holds for every immediate sub-structure (this assumption is the **structural induction hypothesis**) and uses that to deduce $P$ for the object built from those sub-structures.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Claim: Every full binary tree has an odd number of nodes.}
worked_example.description_latex: We begin by recalling the recursive definition we will induct over. A full binary tree is either (i) a single leaf (base case), or (ii) a node with a left full binary tree $T_L$ and a right full binary tree $T_R$ attached (inductive constructor). Setting up this definition is the essential first step because it tells us exactly what our base case and inductive step must cover.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Base case: A single leaf has } 1 \text{ node, and } 1 \text{ is odd.} \checkmark
worked_example.description_latex: We verify the predicate $P(T)$: '$T$ has an odd number of nodes' directly for the smallest possible tree. Since $1$ is odd, the base case is established. This is straightforward but necessary — without it the induction has no foundation.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Inductive hypothesis: Assume } P(T_L) \text{ and } P(T_R): \text{ both } T_L \text{ and } T_R \text{ have an odd number of nodes.}
worked_example.description_latex: Here is the key move that makes structural induction different from standard induction: we assume the predicate holds for BOTH immediate sub-structures, not just a predecessor by size. We are free to assume $P$ for every component that feeds into the constructor, so we write that $|T_L|$ is odd and $|T_R|$ is odd, where $|\cdot|$ denotes node count.

The composite tree $T$ is formed by attaching $T_L$ and $T_R$ to a new root node, so its total node count is the sum of the two sub-tree counts plus one for the root. Substituting the inductive hypothesis, we have odd + odd + 1, and the sum of two odd numbers is even, so adding 1 gives an odd number. Therefore $|T|$ is odd, completing the inductive step.

worked_example.expression_latex: \therefore P(T) \text{ holds for every full binary tree } T. \quad \blacksquare
worked_example.description_latex: By structural induction — we verified the base case and showed the constructor preserves the property — we conclude $P(T)$ holds for every full binary tree. Notice the conclusion follows because the recursive definition covers exactly these two cases and nothing else.

partial_example.expression_latex: \text{Claim: For every full binary tree } T, \text{ the number of leaves equals the number of internal nodes plus 1.}
partial_example.description_latex: We use the same recursive definition as before: a leaf is the base case, and an internal node with sub-trees $T_L, T_R$ is the constructor. Let $\ell(T)$ be the leaf count and $i(T)$ the internal node count. We want to show $\ell(T) = i(T) + 1$ for all $T$.
A single leaf has one leaf and zero internal nodes, so $1 = 0 + 1$ holds. The base case is verified directly with no algebra needed.

Now state the structural induction hypothesis explicitly: what do you assume about $T_L$ and $T_R$? Then use those assumptions to express $\ell(T)$ and $i(T)$ in terms of the sub-tree quantities and derive $\ell(T) = i(T) + 1$. Write out every substitution carefully.

The left sub-tree and right sub-tree each contribute their own leaves (the root itself is internal, not a leaf), so $\ell(T) = \ell(T_L) + \ell(T_R)$. Applying the inductive hypothesis to each and noting $i(T) = i(T_L) + i(T_R) + 1$ (root counts as one internal node) gives the result. Compare your work to this and check each substitution.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: A full binary tree is defined as before. Prove by structural induction that $|T| \geq 1$ for every full binary tree $T$ (i.e., every full binary tree has at least one node).

Let $h(T)$ be the height of a full binary tree (height of a leaf $= 0$, height of an internal node $= 1 + \max(h(T_L), h(T_R))$). Prove by structural induction that $|T| \leq 2^{h(T)+1} - 1$ for every full binary tree $T$.

Define well-formed propositional formulas (WFFs) recursively: (i) every propositional variable $p_i$ is a WFF; (ii) if $\phi$ is a WFF then $\neg\phi$ is a WFF; (iii) if $\phi, \psi$ are WFFs then $(\phi \land \psi)$ is a WFF. Prove by structural induction that every WFF has equal numbers of left and right parentheses.
practice_problems.answer_latex:
ext{Base: a variable } p_i 	ext{ has 0 left and 0 right parens.} \checkmark \ 	ext{Step for } 
eg\phi	ext{: IH gives } \ell(\phi)=r(\phi)	ext{; negation adds none, so } \ell(
eg\phi)=r(
eg\phi). \checkmark \ 	ext{Step for } (\phi \land \psi)	ext{: adds 1 left and 1 right paren, so } \ell = \ell(\phi)+\ell(\psi)+1 = r(\phi)+r(\psi)+1 = r. \checkmark

practice_problems.prompt_latex: Define finite lists over a set $A$ recursively: (i) $[]$ (the empty list) is a finite list; (ii) if $a \in A$ and $L$ is a finite list, then $(a :: L)$ is a finite list. Let $\text{len}(L)$ count elements and $\text{rev}(L)$ reverse the list (with $\text{rev}([]) = []$ and $\text{rev}(a :: L) = \text{rev}(L) ++ [a]$, where $++$ concatenates). Prove by structural induction that $\text{len}(\text{rev}(L)) = \text{len}(L)$ for every finite list $L$. You may use (without proof) that $\text{len}(L_1 ++ L_2) = \text{len}(L_1) + \text{len}(L_2)$.

A compiler writer claims: 'In any arithmetic expression built from integer literals (base case) and the binary operations $+, -, \times$ (each combining two sub-expressions), the number of operation symbols is always exactly one less than the number of integer literals.' Formalize this claim using structural induction and prove it.
practice_problems.answer_latex: \text{Let } \ell(E) = \text{literal count},\, o(E) = \text{operator count.} \\ \text{Claim: } \ell(E) = o(E) + 1. \\ \text{Base: } E = n \text{ (literal): } \ell = 1, o = 0,\, 1 = 0+1. \checkmark \\ \text{Step (IH on } E_1, E_2\text{): } \ell(E_1 \circ E_2) = \ell(E_1)+\ell(E_2) = (o(E_1)+1)+(o(E_2)+1) \\ = o(E_1)+o(E_2)+2 = (o(E_1)+o(E_2)+1)+1 = o(E_1 \circ E_2)+1. \checkmark

common_mistakes: Students apply the inductive hypothesis to only ONE sub-structure when the constructor has two (e.g., assuming $P(T_L)$ but forgetting $P(T_R)$ in a binary tree proof). The fix is to list all immediate sub-components at the start of the inductive step and state the hypothesis for each one explicitly before any algebra begins.

Students confuse the node count formula for a composite tree by writing $|T| = |T_L| + |T_R|$ and forgetting to add 1 for the root (or connector node). Because the root is a new node not belonging to either sub-tree, you must always count it separately: $|T| = |T_L| + |T_R| + 1$.

Students write 'assume the statement holds for all trees of size $n$, then consider size $n+1$' — this is strong induction on size, not structural induction. Structural induction works directly on the shape of the recursive definition without converting to a size parameter; the hypothesis should be stated in terms of the named sub-structures $T_L$ and $T_R$ (or $\phi$ and $\psi$, etc.), not in terms of a number $n$.
untested_variants: Structural induction on $n$-ary trees (where each internal node can have any finite number of children, not just two), requiring an inductive hypothesis over a variable-length list of sub-trees.

Mutual structural induction, where two mutually recursive definitions (e.g., 'even-length lists' and 'odd-length lists' defined in terms of each other) require proving two predicates simultaneously in a single coupled argument.

Using structural induction to prove a property about the evaluation semantics of a simple programming language (e.g., that every expression in a given grammar evaluates to a non-negative integer), where the recursive definition is an inference-rule grammar rather than a tree grammar.

Well-founded induction on a partially-ordered set (such as the subset ordering on finite sets or the divisibility ordering on positive integers), which generalizes structural induction but requires explicitly verifying well-foundedness rather than appealing to a given recursive definition.
